coredata.dc:title: MIL-88A/carbon quantum dots nanomaterials promote the photo-fenton reaction to enhance the fouling resistance of PVDF membrane
coredata.dc:creator: Zheng, Hongai
Li, Mengyao
Jiang, Shuangyan
Xiao, Cunzheng
Zhu, Meilin
Zhou, Yao
Wang, Derui
Sun, Xin
Zhang, Daquan
Zhang, Lizhi
coredata.dc:description: Herein, the MIL-88A/Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) was synthesized by a hydrothermal method, and the polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) membrane modified by the MIL-88A/CQDs was fabricated by vacuum-assisted suction filtration. Compared with the pristine membrane, MIL-88A/CQDs modified membrane based on collaborative photo-Fenton/filtration process shows better removal efficiency for methylene blue (MB), rifampicin (RIF), methyl orange (MO), and Rhodamine B (RhB). In addition, the anti-fouling properties of the modified membrane are significantly improved, and the flux recovery of the MIL-88A/CQDs modified membrane increased from 56% to 89%, and the irreversible fouling ratio (Rir) decreased from 44% to 11%. Significantly, the modified membrane contaminated by MB within 20 min can achieve self-cleaning through the photo-Fenton process. Finally, a possible membrane antifouling mechanism is proposed: superoxide radicals (•O2 -) and hydroxy radicals (•OH) are the main active substances. CQDs accelerates the e− and h+ separation of MIL-88A and promotes the circulation of Fe substances. The fundamental research will present a promising strategy for improving antifouling properties of membrane.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Photo-fenton
PVDF
Membrane fouling
Hydrophilic modification
MIL-88A/CQDs

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Water flux and antifouling ability test

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Research was supported by the Grant from National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 81572218 , 21776172 , 52071198 ), Science and Technology Commission of Shanghai Municipality ( Shanghai Municipal Natural Science Foundation , 13ZR1439100 ) and Shanghai Municipal Health Bureau ( 20124303 ).

Research was supported by the Grant from National Natural Science Foundation of China (81572218, 21776172, 52071198, 52271061), and the Science and Research Project of Shanghai Municipal Health Commission (202240065).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Preparation of CQDs (b) preparation of MIL-88A/CQDs material and modified membranes.
(a) XRD pattern and (b) FT-IR infrared pattern corresponding to MIL-88A, CQDs, MIL-88A/CQDs.

(a) SEM image of MIL-88A/CQDs (b) TEM image of CQDs and (c) MIL-88A/CQDs (d) HR-TEM image of MIL-88A/CQDs (e) Surface view of MIL-88A/CQDs modified membrane(f) Cross section of MIL-88A/CQDs modified membrane and (g) EDS images.
(a) AFM images of the pristine PVDF membrane, (b) MIL-88A/PVDF membrane, (c) MC-5 membrane, (d) MC-10 membrane and (e) MC-20 membrane.
(a) UV–vis DRS spectrum of as-prepared material and (b–d) band gap of CQDs、MIL-88A and MIL-88A/CQDs.
Contact angle of the modified membranes.

(a) Pure water permeability and (b) different pollutant removal efficiency of various modified membranes (T = 298 K; MB concentration = 20 ppm; RhB concentration = 20 ppm; MO concentration = 20 ppm; RIF concentration = 25 ppm; H2O2 = 10 mM, pH = 5).

Normalized curve of BSA flux decline (a); the flux recovery ratio (FRR), total contamination ratio (Rt), irreversible fouling ratio (Rir), and reversible fouling ratio (Rr) of the membrane in different systems (b) (T = 298 K; BSA concentration = 1 g/L; H2O2 = 10 mM; pH = 5).
Removal efficiency of MB by MC-5 modified membrane at different pH (T = 298 K; MB concentration = 20 ppm; H2O2 = 10 mM; pH = 5).
HA and MB degradation efficiency of MC-5 modified membrane under cyclic test (T = 298 K; MB concentration = 20 ppm; HA concentration = 35 ppm; H2O2 = 10 mM; pH = 5).
XPS spectra of (a) survey scan, (b) C 1s, (c) Fe 2p, and (d) O 1s of fresh and used MIL-88A/CQDs.
Self-cleaning properties of MC-5 modified membrane (T = 298 K; MB concentration = 20 ppm; H2O2 = 10 mM; pH = 5).
(a) Effect of quencher on MB removal by MIL-88A/CQDs (b) ESR spectra of MIL-88A/CQDs under dark and visible light conditions.
Schematic diagram of photo-Fenton self-cleaning mechanism of MIL-88A/CQDs membrane in H2O2 - visible light system.

head.ce:title: MIL-88A/carbon quantum dots nanomaterials promote the photo-fenton reaction to enhance the fouling resistance of PVDF membrane

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Environmental and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, the MIL-88A/Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) was synthesized by a hydrothermal method, and the polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) membrane modified by the MIL-88A/CQDs was fabricated by vacuum-assisted suction filtration. Compared with the pristine membrane, MIL-88A/CQDs modified membrane based on collaborative photo-Fenton/filtration process shows better removal efficiency for methylene blue (MB), rifampicin (RIF), methyl orange (MO), and Rhodamine B (RhB). In addition, the anti-fouling properties of the modified membrane are significantly improved, and the flux recovery of the MIL-88A/CQDs modified membrane increased from 56% to 89%, and the irreversible fouling ratio (Rir) decreased from 44% to 11%. Significantly, the modified membrane contaminated by MB within 20 min can achieve self-cleaning through the photo-Fenton process. Finally, a possible membrane antifouling mechanism is proposed: superoxide radicals (•O2 -) and hydroxy radicals (•OH) are the main active substances. CQDs accelerates the e− and h+ separation of MIL-88A and promotes the circulation of Fe substances. The fundamental research will present a promising strategy for improving antifouling properties of membrane.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: With population growth and urban development, the demand for fresh water continues to increase. Therefore, developing efficient and low-consumption wastewater treatment technologies has become crucial. As an efficient technology, membrane separation has certain advantages, such as facile operation, energy saving, and low environmental impact [1]. In terms of membrane materials, polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) membrane, as a kind of polymer membrane, is popular in wastewater purification because of its good chemical stability [2]. But the problem of membrane fouling always exists. There are many ways to solve the problem, such as physical cleaning, chemical cleaning, and membrane modification.

The combination of membrane filtration and photocatalysis technology can impart enhanced self-cleaning properties to the membrane by breaking down contaminants on the membrane surface and changing membrane surface properties, alleviating membrane fouling [3]. At present, many nano-catalysts are used to degrade organic pollutants through the photo-Fenton process [4,5]. The photo-Fenton reaction combines the advantages of photocatalysis with Fenton catalysis. It generates electrons through the photoexcitation of semiconductors and promotes the transformation of Fe3+ to Fe2+, accelerating the photodegradation and decomposition of pollutants. Therefore, developing catalytic materials for photo-Fenton reactions has become a hot and essential topic.

These years, Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) materials have been proven to possess the advantages of adjustable porosity, diverse structure, large specific surface area, and heterogeneous Fenton oxidation as well as photocatalytic properties [6–9]. Fe-based MOF materials have been widely used in visible light catalytic degradation of pollutants and water treatment [10–12]. Due to many Fe3-μ3-oxo clusters, Fe-based MOF can produce hydroxyl radicals and degrade pollutants through the photo-Fenton reaction [13]. In addition, the metal nodes of MOFs can be considered as isolated semiconductor quantum dots, which can be activated directly under light irradiation and act as active sites for other photocatalysts, making Fe-MOFs more suitable for photo-Fenton systems. As one of the widely used Fe-MOFs, MIL-88A has great potential and superiority of simple synthesis, wide pH range, and good stability. In addition, its unique porous crystal structure can provide a large and dispersed active site for other materials and be used for the Fenton reaction to degrade pollutants [14]. Zheng et al. [15] prepared CP/MIL-P/MoS2 multifunctional hydrophilic membrane, which effectively improved the modified membrane's degradation performance and water flux. MIL-88A particles can not only effectively adjust the internal structure of the PVDF membrane but also make the membrane have a higher photo-Fenton self-cleaning ability. Nevertheless, the high photogenerated electron-hole recombination rate greatly limits its development in photocatalysis and water treatment membrane separation.

It has been proved that MIL-88A, coupled with other substances, can inhibit carrier binding and enhance the photocatalytic effect. Liu et al. [16] polymerized ultrathin graphene oxide (GO) on MIL-88A (Fe) surface. The photocatalytic rate of this composite catalyst was greatly improved due to the unique structural advantage of GO, which provided more reaction sites for MOF materials. However, conventional conductive materials do not have sufficient contact with the active site of MIL-88A due to their large size [17]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) has been widely used in photocatalysis due to their excellent biocompatibility and electron transfer ability [18]. At the same time, due to the ultra-small size of CQDs, it can be used as a local electron acceptor and fully contact with MIL-88A active central, thus separating photogenerated electrons more efficiently and easing charge recombination of MOF materials. Lin et al. [17] successfully prepared MIL-53(Fe)/CQD. As a local electron acceptor, CQDs increased the photocatalytic competence of the material effectively. Feng et al. [19] used UIO-66-NH2 and CQD as dopants for the polyether sulfone (PES) ultrafiltration (UF) membrane. They found that CQDs can accelerate electron transfer, thus accelerating the catalysis of pollutants. In addition, its hydrophilicity can enhance the stain resistance of the membrane. However, the research on combining MIL-88A material with CQDs-modified membrane for antifouling membrane treatment has yet to be reported.

In this study, MIL-88A/CQDs modified PVDF membrane was prepared by vacuum-assisted filtration. The basic properties of the materials and the surface morphology of the membranes were studied by different characterization methods. The filtration, antifouling, and degradation properties of the membranes were investigated by flux, self-cleaning, pollutant removal efficiency, flux recovery, and circulation experiments. The antifouling mechanism of the membrane was investigated by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), electrochemical, and electron spin resonance (ESR) tests. This work provides a theoretical reference for the development of surface modified membrane technology.

Fumaric acid (C4H4O4), ferric chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O), isopropanol (IPA), benzoquinone (BQ), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), NaOH, HCl, sodium ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na), methylene blue (MB), humic acid (HA) and bovine serum albumin (BSA) were obtained from Shanghai Titan Scientific Co. (China). Urea (99%) was obtained from Shanghai MacLean Biochemical Co., LTD. Aladdin Industrial Corporation supplied citrate (CA) (99.5%). PVDF membranes were purchased from Tianjin Jinteng Technology Co., LTD. The membrane diameter is 50 mm, and the pore diameter is 0.22 μm.

1 g citrate (CA) and 1 g urea were mixed with 25 mL deionized water and placed in a hydrothermal kettle. The reaction was carried out in a vacuum oven at 180 °C for 4 h and then cooled to 25 °C. The solution was then centrifuged, and the resulting clarified solution was injected into a dialysis bag for 24 h of dialysis, with deionized water changed every 8 h. Finally, the dialysate was dried to obtain CQDs.

The MIL-88A was prepared based on the literature reports [20]. 0.005 mol FeCl3·6H2O and 0.005 mol C4H4O4 were added into 25 mL deionized water to dissolve evenly and transferred the solution to a hydrothermal kettle for 12 h reaction. The mixture was then centrifuged three times with deionized water and anhydrous ethanol. After that, the solid was dried to obtain a powder.

The preparation of MIL-88A/CQDs was according to the literature [1]. Different masses of CQDs (0 mg, 5 mg, 10 mg, 20 mg) and 10 mg MIL-88A solid powder were placed in 30 mL of deionized water to form a uniformly mixed solution. The other steps were the same as the preparation of MIL-88A.

The MIL-88A/CQDs modified membranes were prepared by vacuum-assisted suction filtration. The preliminary treatment of the PVDF membrane was based on the literature [21]. The 5 g catalyst was dissolved in 5 mL deionized water for the ultrasound. Then the solution was loaded on the PVDF membrane, and 5 mL polyethylene glycol (PEG, 0.25 g/mL) and 5 mL glutaraldehyde (GA, 25% aqueous solution) were successively filtered on the modified membrane as a cross-linking agent to prepare MIL-88A/CQDs modification membrane. The modified membranes with different mixing ratios were named MC-5, MC-10, and MC-20 according to the content of CQDs in the catalyst preparation, the membrane without the loaded catalyst was named as M0. The modification process is shown in Fig. 1 a and b. The catalyst compositions of the different membranes are shown in Table S1.

Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR, PerkinElmer, USA) was used to investigate composites' functional groups. The crystal structure of the composite material was determined by an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Bruker-D8, Germany). Atomic Force Microscopy (AFM, Bruker Dimension ICON, Germany) was used to characterize the membrane surface morphology. The photoluminescence spectra (PL) of the photocatalyst were measured by a fluorescence spectrometer (RF-5301 PC). Changes in material content before and after use were analyzed using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Fisher Technology K-Alpha, USA). The optical absorption properties of the material were obtained by the solid ultraviolet–visible diffuse reflection spectra (UV-2550, SHIMADZU, Japan), and the optical band gap of the material was calculated by the Tauc equation (1).
(1)
(
α h v
)
=
A
(
hv −
E g
)
n / 2
Where α is the absorption index, h is Planck's constant, v is the frequency; A is a constant, and Eg is the band gap.

The membrane properties were measured using a dead-end filtration device, and the transmembrane pressure was controlled at a constant 0.04 MPa. The pure water flux determination method was as follows: the volume of the modified membrane filtrate was set to be constant, and the time for different modified membranes to reach a certain volume of the filtrate was different. The permeate flux of the modified membrane was calculated from formula 2.
(2)
J =
V
A × P × t
In the formula, V is the permeation volume (L), A (m2) is the effective area of the membrane (cm2), t represents the operating time, and P (bar) is the operating pressure.

A 300 W xenon light source (λ ≥ 420 nm, 520 mW/cm2) was used as a visible light source to irradiate the surface of the membrane (12.56 cm2) for photo-Fenton dye removal. The membrane was 10 cm from the light source. The dye concentration in the feed solution was 20 mg/L, and H2O2 was 10 mM, and the filtrate was collected by gravity settling, and the absorbance was measured. The formula for separation efficiency is as follows.
(3)
R =
(
1 −
C f
C 0
)
Among them, Cf represents the concentration of the feed liquid, and C0 is the concentration of the filtrate.

BSA (1.0 g/L) was selected as the model fouling to study the modified membranes' antifouling properties further. First, the pure water flux (J1) was measured, followed by changing the feed solution to BSA solution and measuring the fouling water flux (J2) at a pressure of 0.04 MPa. Subsequently, the modified membrane was subjected to photo-Fenton cleaning, and 20 mL of deionized water and 10 mM of H2O2 were added to wash the fouled membrane with stirring at 300 rpm. After cleaning, the membrane's pure water flux (J3) was measured again. The flux recovery ratio (FRR) and total pollution ratio (Rt) can be calculated by formulas (4) and (5).
(4)
F R R =
J 3
J 1
× 100 %
(5)
R t
=
J 1
−
J 2
J 1
× 100 %

Among them, reversible fouling ratio (Rr) and irreversible fouling ratio (Rir) are calculated by the following equation.
(6)
R r
=
J 3
−
J 2
J 1
× 100 %
(7)
R
i r
=
J 1
−
J 3
J 1
× 100 %
The measurements were repeated at least three times.

Membrane flux stability and reproducibility were tested with a dead-end filter device. The stability and reusability of membrane separation properties were evaluated using a vacuum filter. With 35 mg/L HA as a model pollutant, the filtration, and water flux tests were the same as above. A total of five groups were performed, and each group was tested three times to reduce experiments error. The self-cleaning property of the membrane was tested by ultraviolet spectrophotometer. In the self-cleaning experiment, 20 mg/L MB was filtered through the modified membrane by gravity sedimentation, each group lasted for 12 h. After that, the contaminated membrane was placed in a beaker with 20 mL of deionized water and stirred under low light for 20 min before the absorbance was tested. A self-cleaning experiment was then carried out under a xenon lamp to test the concentration of residual pollutants every 5 min.

Fig. 2 shows a graphical of the prepared materials' chemical composition and lattice structure. It can be seen from Fig. 2a that the XRD pattern of MIL-88A has two characteristic diffraction peaks at 2θ = 10.3° and 2θ = 12.8°, respectively. According to the literature [22], they correspond to the (101) crystal plane and the (002) crystal plane, respectively. The diffraction peak intensity is very strong, which indicates that the MIL-88A has good crystallinity [23]. The XRD pattern of CQDs shows a diffraction peak centered at 23.8°, possibly due to the disordered graphite-like substances contained in CQDs. The low-intensity diffraction peaks are more related to amorphous carbon. The composite exhibits good characteristic diffraction peaks and can correspond well to the individual MIL-88A and CQDs, indicating the successful preparation of MIL-88A/CQDs. Fig. 2b shows the FT-IR spectrum of the obtained material. It can be seen that the vibrational peaks of MIL-88A at 1390, 996, and 690 cm−1 correspond to –COOH, Fe–O, Fe–O–H, respectively [12,24]. The 3200-3700 cm−1 range represents the O–H stretching vibration of the MIL-88A surface. A broad absorption band is observed at 3281 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectrum of CQDs, mainly due to the stretching vibration of amino and hydroxyl groups [25]. A sharp peak is observed at 1708 cm−1, corresponding to the CO structure [26]. The 1430 cm−1 corresponds to the CC structure, and the 1220 cm−1 corresponds to the C–O–H structure [27]. In the spectrum of the MIL-88A/CQDs sample, a new characteristic peak at 1565 cm−1 corresponds to C–O in CQDs [28]. Furthermore, in the composite, the absorption band located at 1390 cm−1 corresponds to the symmetric vibration of fumaric acid, which is more intense than MIL-88A [29]. This is mainly due to the introduction of CQDs, which is functionalized by many oxygen-containing functional groups (-COOH, –COOR, etc.).

The morphology of the composite can be observed by SEM and TEM. MIL-88A exhibits a well-crystallized hexagonal pyramidal structure with an average length of 2–5 μm [14]. As shown in Fig. 3 , due to the small size of CQDs, the CQDs prepared by TEM characterization can be seen in Fig. 3b with a large number of small size CQDs, and their size distribution is in the range of 2–2.5 nm [30]. Fig. 3c shows the TEM image of MIL-88A/CQDs. Fig. 3d displays the HR-TEM image of MIL-88A/CQDs at high magnification. The lattice spacings of MIL-88A and CQDs correspond to the (011) and (100) planes, illustrating the successful preparation of the composite material. At the same time, Fig. 3e and f clearly show the presence of materials on the membrane and in the pore size, indicating that the composite material is successfully combined with the PVDF membrane.

The roughness of the pristine and modified membranes is analyzed by AFM. It can be seen from Fig. 4 that the surface roughness of all modified membranes is reduced compared with the pristine membrane. After loading MIL-88A, the roughness of the modified membranes is lower than that of the pristine membrane, which can be attributed to the increase of hydrophilic functional groups (including N and O), and the construction of hydrophilic micro-nano hierarchy [29]. From Fig. 4c–e, it can be seen that the roughness of the modified membrane decreases with the increasing amount of CQDs. As can be seen from the figures, the reduced roughness of the MIL-88A/CQDs-modified membrane may be caused by filling the membrane surface gullies with the CQDs.

UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy can reflect the optical properties of materials. Fig. 5 a shows that MIL-88A exhibits a significant absorbance at 300–500 nm. The strong adsorption of pure MIL-88A is attributed to the presence of Fe–O clusters [14]. However, CQDs exhibits strong light absorption in the range of 300–800 nm, possibly due to the photoluminescence peculiarity that makes the material to exhibit strong absorption of visible light. MIL-88A/CQDs composites of different proportions exhibit enhanced light absorption to a certain extent. Due to the photoluminescence characteristics of CQDs, the light absorption intensity increases with the proportion of CQDs [31]. Therefore, MIL-88A/CQDs exhibits better light-harvesting efficiency and stronger photo-Fenton activity than MIL-88A alone. The calculation results of MIL-88A and MIL-88A/CQD bandgap energy are shown in Fig. 5b–d. The band-gap energy of MIL-88A/CQDs (2.53 eV) is lower than that of MIL-88A (2.79 eV), indicating that the addition of CQDs can enhance the light absorption. The photocatalytic activity of the composites is further improved.

Fig. 6 shows a diagram of a material electrochemical test performed on a three-electrode system electrochemical workstation. The charge transfer resistance of the catalyst is observed by the EIS spectrum. In the EIS spectrum, the arc radius represents the charge transfer resistance. The smaller the arc radius means faster the charge transfer [32]. It can be seen from Fig. 6a that MIL-88A/CQDs has the fastest charge transfer. In addition, the PL spectrum reflects the strength of the recombination ability of photogenerated electrons and holes. The stronger the PL characteristic peak is, the higher the carrier recombination rate is presented [32]. Fig. 6b shows that the emission peaks of MIL-88A/CQDs are weaker than those of MIL-88A, indicating a low carrier recombination rate. The above results illustrate that the introduction of CQDs into MIL-88A can effectively promote the charge transfer resistance of the catalyst, reduce the carrier recombination rate, and improve the photo-Fenton reaction activity.

Theoretically, the improvement of hydrophilicity can effectively enhance the antifouling performance of the membrane [33]. In Fig. 7 , the contact angle of the unmodified membrane is 54.8°, and the M0 contact angle is 54.3°, indicating that the crosslinking agent has little effect on the membrane. After loading MIL-88A on the surface, the contact angle decreases significantly to 21.4°, which may be due to the hydrophilic functional group of MIL-88A [34]. On the other hand, after incorporating CQDs, the contact angles demonstrate different degrees of decrease. It can be seen that the contact angles decrease with the increasing in the incorporation of CQDs. According to the literature [35], it is attributed to the addition of hydrophilic CQDs to effectively connect the adjacent nanomaterials, thereby reducing the membrane's pore size and enhancing the water transport path, which proves that the as-prepared modified membrane has improving hydrophilicity [36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water flux and antifouling ability test
ce:section.ce:para: The pure water flux of the membranes is tested in Fig. 8 a. Among them, the pure water flux of the M0 membrane is 918 L⸳m−2 ⸳h−1 ⸳bar−1. The hydrophilicity of the MIL-88A-modified membrane decreases, which may be caused by some materials blocking the membrane pores during the operation of the membrane module. Compared with the MIL-88A modified membrane, the pure water flux of MIL-88A/CQDs modified membranes increase, mainly because the hydrophilicity of intercalated CQDs enhances the transport path of water molecules, improves the hydrophilicity of the membrane and reduces the transport resistance of water molecules [37]. However, the water flux tends to decline with the increase of CQDs. The decreasing fluxes of MC-10 and MC-20 may be caused by the aggregation of MIL-88A/CQDs nanoparticles and their uneven distribution on the PVDF membrane surface [28,38]. According to Fig. S1, the porosity of the membranes is first increased and then decreased after modification by MIL-88A/CQDs materials, which may lead to an increase and then decrease in water flux. Meanwhile, the modified membranes have a higher removal ability of dye and drug pollutants than the pristine membrane. Compared with the MIL-88A modified membrane, the MC-5 modified membrane shows better pure water flux and dye-removing performance. The main reason is that the incorporation of CQDs accelerates the electron transport of MIL-88A and improves the removal effect of pollutants through the photo-Fenton reaction. However, the anti-fouling performance of MC-10 and MC-20 decrease. The reason may be that the content of CQDs is too high, and the content of MIL-88A is not enough to degrade pollutants through the photo-Fenton reaction. Therefore, the MC-5 modified membrane is used for testing in subsequent experiments.

Fouling reduces the membrane performance and shortens membrane life, which is one of the inevitable problems of filter membranes [39]. Membrane fouling is divided into Rr and Rir. In the study of the antifouling performance of the modified membrane, 1 g/L BSA solution is selected as the model contaminant. The FRR, Rt, Rr, and Rir are calculated, and the results are shown in Fig. 9 . The FRR and Rir of the M0 membrane are 56% and 44%, respectively, indicating that BSA fouling severely. A high FRR value represents a good antifouling property of the membrane. It can be seen that the FRR of all modified membranes are higher than that of the pristine membrane. Among them, MC-5 modified membrane shows the largest FRR value, because the combination of a certain amount of CQDs and MIL-88A can remove most of the pollutants gathered on the membrane through photo-Fenton reaction during the membrane cleaning process. On the other hand, compared with M0, the irreversible fouling of MC-5 modified membrane gradually reduces to 11%, indicating that the photocatalyst supported on the surface of the membrane plays a crucial role in the photo-Fenton cleaning process. However, with the increase of the proportion of CQDs, we find that the FRR of MC-10 and MC-20 decreases and the Rir of them increases. This indicates that with the increase of CQDs, the antifouling property of the modified membrane decreases to a certain extent. This may be that too many CQDs accumulate on the surface and pores of the MIL-88A material, blocking the light absorption of the catalyst and thus reducing the removal of contaminants [40,41].

The effect of pH on the Fenton reaction was investigated. The reaction was first performed at natural pH, tested without adjustment to approximately 5. Then, comparative experiments were performed, using aqueous HCl (1 mol/L) and aqueous NaOH (1 mol/L) to adjust the pH of the solution. As can be observed in Fig. 10 , the highest degradation efficiency is achieved when the pH reaches 4. The removal efficiency of MB by the modified membrane remains above 76% for pH values in the range of 2–9, indicating that the modified membrane has good adaptability to different pH. MB and HA are used as model contaminants to test the cycling performance of the membrane. The modified membranes are tested for 5 cycles, each cycle lasting 40 min, and the results are shown in Fig. 11 . After 5 cycles, the retention efficiency of HA of the modified membrane still maintains at about 90%. At the same time, the removal efficiency of MB is stable. Compared with the first cycle, the removal efficiency of MB decreases slightly after the 5th cycle, indicating that the modified membranes have excellent long-term operation ability. The results show that the prepared MIL-88A/CQDs modified membranes can effectively reduce the membrane fouling.

To elucidate the oxidation states of the MIL-88A/CQDs, XPS tests are carried out on the materials before and after the reaction, and the results are shown in Fig. 12 . The peaks of Fe, O, C, and other elements appear in the samples, indicating that these elements coexist in the materials. The C 1s spectrum of the sample is divided into three peaks at 284.78, 286.48, and 288.27 eV, corresponding to C–C, CO, C–O, and CO of the carbon-containing functional groups [42]. Fig. 12c shows the XPS spectrum of Fe 2p, and Fe 2p3/2 is deconvoluted into three peaks at 710.1, 713.6, and 717.6 eV. The main peak obtained at 723.9 eV which corresponds to Fe 2p1/2. Compared with the material after the reaction, the binding energy of Fe 2p does not change much; about 6.8% of Fe (Ⅱ) is converted to Fe (Ⅲ), indicating that the addition of CQDs promotes the Fe (Ⅱ)/Fe (Ⅲ) cycle. The results show that CQDs as electron donors can achieve a good redox cycle of Fe, accelerate the decomposition process of H2O2, and further promote the degradation of pollutants.

The pollutants attached to the membrane will seriously damage the separation performance and permeability of the modified membrane. Traditional cleaning methods will cause re-contamination of the membrane [37]. The self-cleaning performance of the membrane is tested through the visible photo-Fenton process. The contaminated membrane is added to the H2O2 solution and photo-Fenton self-cleaning experiments are performed under a xenon lamp. The results are shown in Fig. 13 . The remaining MB dye molecules on the membrane are removed within 20 min. The results show that MIL-88A/CQDs membranes have strong photo-Fenton self-cleaning properties and can quickly remove dyes. In addition, after 5 cycles, the self-cleaning performance and surface morphology of the modified membrane do not change significantly.

The active species in the photo-Fenton reaction are explored by free radical quenching experiments and ESR tests. Benzoquinone (BQ), isopropanol (IPA), and EDTA-2Na are used as scavengers, quenching •O2 -, •OH, h+ [35]. Fig. 14 a shows that when BQ and IPA are present, the removal efficiency of MB decreases substantially. This indicates that •O2 - and •OH are the main active species during the photo-Fenton reaction. This corresponds to the results in Fig. 14b. As seen from the ESR map, there is no characteristic peak in the control group under dark condition, indicating no production of active species.

Based on the previous photo-Fenton research, a possible photo-Fenton mechanism for the antifouling of the MIL-88A/CQDs membrane surface under the visible light system is proposed, which is shown in Fig. 15 . Under the excitation of visible light, the MIL-88A/CQDs membranes generate e− and h+ pairs in the MIL-88A semiconductor. The reduced e− can react with the oxygen in the air to generate oxidatively active •O2 -. At the same time, oxidized h+ can directly oxidize pollutants. Besides, the visible light absorption capability of MIL-88A is heightened because of the presence of conductive CQDs. More importantly, e− and h+ in MIL-88A semiconductors are rapidly separated due to the existence of CQDs. The presence of e− promotes the redox cycle of Fe (Ⅱ) and Fe (Ⅲ) in MIL-88A and accelerates the formation of •OH. High photo-Fenton catalytic activity can rapidly degrade pollutants, ensuring the membrane's high flux and pollutant removal efficiency. The proposed reactions are presented as following:
(8) CQDs/MIL-88A + hv →e-+ h+
(9) H2O2+e−→ •OH + OH−
(10) e-+ O2 → •O2 -
(11) Fe3++H2O2→Fe2++HO2•+H+
(12) Fe2++H2O2→Fe3++ •OH + OH−
(13) •OH/•O2 −/h++Pollutants→Products

In this paper, MIL-88A/CQDs modified PVDF membranes were fabricated by vacuum filtration. SEM results indicate that MIL-88A presents a hexagonal pyramid shape, and MIL-88A/CQDs can also stably adhere to the PVDF membrane. Compared with the pristine membrane, MIL-88A/CQDs membrane improves the removal efficiency of MB, RhB, MO and RIF; and have good antifouling properties with flux recovery up to 89%. Under light irradiation, the MIL-88A/CQDs membrane exhibits an excellent antifouling property. After several experiments, the removal efficiency of HA and MB can be maintained at more than 90% and about 80% respectively. Membrane antifouling mechanism analysis results display that •O2 - and •OH have great effects on the degradation of pollutants. The CQDs accelerates the e− and h+ separation process efficiency of MIL-88A while promoting the recycling of Fe species. In the meantime, the hydrophilicity of MIL-88A/CQDs and the photo-Fenton reaction synergistically enhance the antifouling effect of the PVDF membrane.

Hongai Zheng is the conceptualizer and leader of the project, guiding the experimental design, and data analysis, and participated in the writing and revision of the manuscript; Mengyao Li completed the experimental study, data analysis, and writing of the original manuscript; Shuangyan Jiang and Meilin Zhu supervised the project and participated in the discussion of the project and provided language help; and the other collaborators assisted with the experimental and analytical studies and contributed equivalently to this project.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Research was supported by the Grant from National Natural Science Foundation of China (81572218, 21776172, 52071198, 52271061), and the Science and Research Project of Shanghai Municipal Health Commission (202240065).
